comment_text.snp3.6:16.1: The Commentary allows kappa in the sense of both “mental activity” and “eon” here. The context in this verse suggests it is about the eons of transmigration.
comment_text.snp3.6:19.5: Commentary glosses brahmā with brāhmaṇa, which is required by the sense.
comment_text.snp3.6:24.1: I think Norman’s reconstruction of this to root ñā (followed by Bodhi) is too speculative. The word makes fine sense as is, and is more metaphorically connected with the sense of khetta.
comment_text.snp3.6:25.1: Reading vijeyya; both viceyya and vijeyya are accepted in the commentary. The “fields” are the “fields of deeds” (AN 3.77:2.3).
comment_text.snp3.6:27.1: Paṇḍarāni means not “senses” per commentary, but is a synonym for suddhi in the next line. A true paṇḍita is pure both inside and out.
comment_text.snp3.6:33.2: The commentary’s “abode of energy” for viriyavāso doesn’t sound right to me, though followed by Bodhi and Norman. I read viriyavā so.
comment_text.snp3.6:37.1: The absolutives sutvā and abhiññāya apply to their respective following accusatives sabbadhammaṁ and sāvajjānavajjaṁ.
comment_text.snp3.6:38.1: Here ālaya (“clinging”) is punned with ariya (“noble”). See MN 39:28.1.
comment_text.snp3.6:38.3: The three perceptions are sensuality, malice, and cruelty. Since paṅka is a stock metaphor for kāma, it seems we should read “the bog of the three perceptions” rather than “the three perceptions and the bog”.
comment_text.snp3.6:39.1: The syntax reads more cleanly if the nominatives in this line and the next are read together; and the clauses following sabb- are likewise read in parallel.
comment_text.snp3.6:40.5: Pariyanta means “limit, boundary”. An arahant still has name & form, but only until the end of their life.
comment_text.snp3.6:40.6: I think pattipatta is a play on words here.
comment_text.snp3.6:42.1: These are the sixty-two views of the Brahmajālasutta (DN 1). Substantialist view underlies them all (SN 41.3:4.13), and according to the commentary it is here included as the sixty-third. Numbered as “sixty” at SN 8.2:5.1 = Thag 21.1:9.1.
comment_text.snp3.6:44.4: Oddly, at AN 5.38:5.4 we find sorata with sakhila in a similar sense to akhila here.
comment_text.snp3.6:47.4: A rare Pali reference to the legendary sages Nārada and his nephew Parvata, whose story is in the Viṣṇu Purāṇa.

root_text.snp3.6:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā rājagahe viharati veḷuvane kalandakanivāpe.
root_text.snp3.6:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena sabhiyassa paribbājakassa purāṇasālohitāya devatāya pañhā uddiṭṭhā honti:
root_text.snp3.6:1.4: “yo te, sabhiya, samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā ime pañhe puṭṭho byākaroti tassa santike brahmacariyaṁ careyyāsī”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:2.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako tassā devatāya santike te pañhe uggahetvā ye te samaṇabrāhmaṇā saṅghino gaṇino gaṇācariyā ñātā yasassino titthakarā sādhusammatā bahujanassa, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.snp3.6:2.2: pūraṇo kassapo makkhaligosālo ajito kesakambalo pakudho kaccāno sañcayo belaṭṭhaputto nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto—
root_text.snp3.6:2.4: Te sabhiyena paribbājakena pañhe puṭṭhā na sampāyanti;
root_text.snp3.6:3.2: “ye kho te bhonto samaṇabrāhmaṇā saṅghino gaṇino gaṇācariyā ñātā yasassino titthakarā sādhusammatā bahujanassa, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.snp3.6:3.4: te mayā pañhe puṭṭhā na sampāyanti,
root_text.snp3.6:4.2: “ayampi kho samaṇo gotamo saṅghī ceva gaṇī ca gaṇācariyo ca ñāto yasassī titthakaro sādhusammato bahujanassa;
root_text.snp3.6:4.3: yannūnāhaṁ samaṇaṁ gotamaṁ upasaṅkamitvā ime pañhe puccheyyan”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:5.2: “yepi kho te bhonto samaṇabrāhmaṇā jiṇṇā vuḍḍhā mahallakā addhagatā vayoanuppattā therā rattaññū cirapabbajitā saṅghino gaṇino gaṇācariyā ñātā yasassino titthakarā sādhusammatā bahujanassa, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.snp3.6:5.4: tepi mayā pañhe puṭṭhā na sampāyanti,
root_text.snp3.6:5.5: asampāyantā kopañca dosañca appaccayañca pātukaronti, api ca maññevettha paṭipucchanti;
root_text.snp3.6:5.6: kiṁ pana me samaṇo gotamo ime pañhe puṭṭho byākarissati.
root_text.snp3.6:5.7: Samaṇo hi gotamo daharo ceva jātiyā, navo ca pabbajjāyā”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:6.2: “samaṇo kho daharoti na uññātabbo na paribhotabbo.
root_text.snp3.6:6.3: Daharopi cesa samaṇo gotamo mahiddhiko hoti mahānubhāvo, yannūnāhaṁ samaṇaṁ gotamaṁ upasaṅkamitvā ime pañhe puccheyyan”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:7.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako yena rājagahaṁ tena cārikaṁ pakkāmi.
root_text.snp3.6:7.2: Anupubbena cārikaṁ caramāno yena rājagahaṁ veḷuvanaṁ kalandakanivāpo, yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavatā saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.snp3.6:7.3: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi.
root_text.snp3.6:7.4: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho sabhiyo paribbājako bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi—
root_text.snp3.6:11.3: Yaṁ vatāhaṁ aññesu samaṇabrāhmaṇesu okāsakammamattampi nālatthaṁ taṁ me idaṁ samaṇena gotamena okāsakammaṁ katan”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:11.4: Attamano pamudito udaggo pītisomanassajāto bhagavantaṁ pañhaṁ apucchi—
root_text.snp3.6:14.4: Ussadā yassa na santi sorato so.
root_text.snp3.6:17.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā attamano pamudito udaggo pītisomanassajāto bhagavantaṁ uttariṁ pañhaṁ apucchi—
root_text.snp3.6:23.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako …pe… bhagavantaṁ uttariṁ pañhaṁ apucchi—
root_text.snp3.6:29.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako …pe… bhagavantaṁ uttariṁ pañhaṁ apucchi—
root_text.snp3.6:35.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako …pe… bhagavantaṁ uttariṁ pañhaṁ apucchi—
root_text.snp3.6:39.4: Paṭighā yassa na santi caraṇavā so.
root_text.snp3.6:41.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako bhagavato bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā attamano pamudito udaggo pītisomanassajāto uṭṭhāyāsanā ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā yena bhagavā tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā bhagavantaṁ sammukhā sāruppāhi gāthāhi abhitthavi:
root_text.snp3.6:52.1: Atha kho sabhiyo paribbājako bhagavato pādesu sirasā nipatitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.snp3.6:52.3: esāhaṁ bhagavantaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāmi dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca;
root_text.snp3.6:52.4: labheyyāhaṁ, bhante, bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ, labheyyaṁ upasampadan”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:53.1: “Yo kho, sabhiya, aññatitthiyapubbo imasmiṁ dhammavinaye ākaṅkhati pabbajjaṁ, ākaṅkhati upasampadaṁ, so cattāro māse parivasati; catunnaṁ māsānaṁ accayena āraddhacittā bhikkhū pabbājenti, upasampādenti bhikkhubhāvāya.
root_text.snp3.6:54.1: “Sace, bhante, aññatitthiyapubbā imasmiṁ dhammavinaye ākaṅkhantā pabbajjaṁ, ākaṅkhantā upasampadaṁ cattāro māse parivasanti, catunnaṁ māsānaṁ accayena āraddhacittā bhikkhū pabbājenti, upasampādenti bhikkhubhāvāya, ahaṁ cattāri vassāni parivasissāmi; catunnaṁ vassānaṁ accayena āraddhacittā bhikkhū pabbājentu upasampādentu bhikkhubhāvāyā”ti.
root_text.snp3.6:54.2: Alattha kho sabhiyo paribbājako bhagavato santike pabbajjaṁ alattha upasampadaṁ …pe…
root_text.snp3.6:54.3: aññataro kho panāyasmā sabhiyo arahataṁ ahosīti.

translation_text.snp3.6:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
translation_text.snp3.6:1.3: Now at that time the wanderer Sabhiya had been presented with several questions by a deity who was a former blood relation, saying:
translation_text.snp3.6:1.4: “Sabhiya, you should live the spiritual life with whatever ascetic or brahmin answers these questions.”
translation_text.snp3.6:2.1: Then Sabhiya, after learning those questions in the presence of that deity, approached those ascetics and brahmins who led an order and a community, and taught a community, who were well-known and famous religious founders, deemed holy by many people. That is,
translation_text.snp3.6:2.2: Pūraṇa Kassapa, the bamboo-staffed ascetic Gosāla, Ajita of the hair blanket, Pakudha Kaccāyana, Sañjaya Belaṭṭhiputta, and the Jain ascetic of the Ñātika clan.
translation_text.snp3.6:2.3: And he asked them those questions,
translation_text.snp3.6:2.4: but they were stumped by them.
translation_text.snp3.6:3.6: they questioned me in return on that matter.
translation_text.snp3.6:3.7: Why don’t I return to a lesser life so I can enjoy sensual pleasures?”
translation_text.snp3.6:4.2: “This ascetic Gotama also leads an order and a community, and tutors a community. He’s a well-known and famous religious founder, deemed holy by many people.
translation_text.snp3.6:4.3: Why don’t I ask him this question?”
translation_text.snp3.6:5.2: “Even those ascetics and brahmins who are elderly and senior, who are advanced in years and have reached the final stage of life; who are senior, long standing, long gone forth; who lead an order and a community, and tutor a community; who are well-known and famous religious founders, deemed holy by many people—that is
translation_text.snp3.6:5.5: They displayed annoyance, hate, and bitterness, and even questioned me in return.
translation_text.snp3.6:5.6: How can the ascetic Gotama possibly answer my questions,
translation_text.snp3.6:5.7: since he is so young in age and newly gone forth?”
translation_text.snp3.6:6.2: “An ascetic should not be looked down upon or disparaged because they are young.
translation_text.snp3.6:6.3: Though young, the ascetic Gotama has great psychic power and might. Why don’t I ask him this question?”
translation_text.snp3.6:7.1: Then Sabhiya set out for Rājagaha.
translation_text.snp3.6:7.2: Traveling stage by stage, he came to Rājagaha, the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground. He went up to the Buddha and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.snp3.6:7.3: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side,
translation_text.snp3.6:7.4: and addressed the Buddha in verse:
translation_text.snp3.6:8.1: “I’ve come full of  doubts and uncertainties,”
translation_text.snp3.6:8.5: Answer my questions in turn, <j>in accordance with the truth.”
translation_text.snp3.6:9.4: I shall solve them for you,
translation_text.snp3.6:9.5: answering your questions in turn, <j>in accordance with the truth.
translation_text.snp3.6:11.3: Where those other ascetics and brahmins didn’t even give me a chance, the Buddha has invited me.”
translation_text.snp3.6:11.4: Uplifted and elated, full of rapture and happiness, he asked this question.
translation_text.snp3.6:12.1: “What must one attain to be called a mendicant?”
translation_text.snp3.6:12.3: “How is one ‘gentle’, how said to be ‘tamed’?
translation_text.snp3.6:12.4: How is one declared to be ‘awakened’?
translation_text.snp3.6:12.5: May the Buddha please answer my question.”
translation_text.snp3.6:13.1: “When by the path they have walked themselves,”
translation_text.snp3.6:13.4: giving up desire for existence or nonexistence,
translation_text.snp3.6:13.5: their journey complete, their rebirths ended: <j>that is a mendicant.
translation_text.snp3.6:14.2: they don’t harm anyone in the world.
translation_text.snp3.6:14.3: An ascetic who has crossed over, unclouded,
translation_text.snp3.6:14.4: who has no pretensions, is gentle.
translation_text.snp3.6:15.2: inside and out in the whole world.
translation_text.snp3.6:15.3: Having pierced through this world and the next,
translation_text.snp3.6:16.1: They have examined the eons in their entirety,
translation_text.snp3.6:16.2: and both sides of transmigration—<j>passing away and rebirth.
translation_text.snp3.6:16.4: the one they call ‘awakened’ has attained <j>the end of rebirth.”
translation_text.snp3.6:17.1: And then, having approved and agreed with what the Buddha said, uplifted and elated, full of rapture and happiness, Sabhiya asked another question:
translation_text.snp3.6:18.1: “What must one attain to be called ‘brahmin’?”
translation_text.snp3.6:18.3: “Why is one an ‘ascetic’, and how a ‘bathed initiate’?
translation_text.snp3.6:18.4: How is one declared to be a ‘giant’?
translation_text.snp3.6:18.5: May the Buddha please answer my question.”
translation_text.snp3.6:20.1: An assuaged one who has given up good and evil,
translation_text.snp3.6:20.2: stainless, understanding this world and the next,
translation_text.snp3.6:21.1: Having washed off all bad things
translation_text.snp3.6:21.2: inside and out in the whole world,
translation_text.snp3.6:21.3: among gods and humans bound to creations,
translation_text.snp3.6:21.4: the one they call ‘washed’ does not return to creation.
translation_text.snp3.6:22.1: They do nothing monstrous at all in the world,
translation_text.snp3.6:23.1: And then Sabhiya asked another question:
translation_text.snp3.6:24.1: “Who is a ‘field-victor’ according to the Buddhas?”
translation_text.snp3.6:24.3: “Why is one ‘skillful’, and how ‘a wise scholar’?
translation_text.snp3.6:24.4: How is one declared to be a ‘sage’?
translation_text.snp3.6:24.5: May the Buddha please answer my question.”
translation_text.snp3.6:25.1: “They are victors on the fields of deeds in their entirety,”
translation_text.snp3.6:25.3: “the fields of gods, humans, and divinitiess;
translation_text.snp3.6:25.4: released from the root bondage to all fields:
translation_text.snp3.6:26.1: They have examined the coffers in their entirety,
translation_text.snp3.6:26.2: the coffers of gods, humans, and divinitiess;
translation_text.snp3.6:26.3: released from the root bondage to all coffers:
translation_text.snp3.6:27.2: both inside and out; understanding purity,
translation_text.snp3.6:27.3: they have left dark and bright behind:
translation_text.snp3.6:27.4: one such is rightly called ‘a wise scholar’.”
translation_text.snp3.6:28.1: Understanding the nature of the bad and the good
translation_text.snp3.6:28.2: inside and out in the whole world;
translation_text.snp3.6:28.3: one worthy of honor by gods and humans,
translation_text.snp3.6:28.4: who has escaped from the net and the chain: that is a sage.”
translation_text.snp3.6:29.1: And then Sabhiya asked another question:
translation_text.snp3.6:30.1: “What must one attain <j>to be called ‘knowledge master’?”
translation_text.snp3.6:30.3: “Why is one ‘studied’, and how is one ‘heroic’?
translation_text.snp3.6:30.5: May the Buddha please answer my question.”
translation_text.snp3.6:31.1: “They have examined knowledges in their entirety,”
translation_text.snp3.6:31.3: “those that are current among ascetics and brahmins;
translation_text.snp3.6:31.4: rid of greed for all feelings,
translation_text.snp3.6:31.5: having left all knowledges behind: <j>that is a knowledge master.
translation_text.snp3.6:32.1: Having studied proliferation and name & form
translation_text.snp3.6:32.2: inside and out—the root of disease;
translation_text.snp3.6:32.3: released from the root bondage to all disease:
translation_text.snp3.6:33.2: heroic, he escapes from the suffering of hell;
translation_text.snp3.6:34.2: the root of clinging inside and out;
translation_text.snp3.6:34.3: released from the root bondage to all clinging:
translation_text.snp3.6:35.1: And then Sabhiya asked another question:
translation_text.snp3.6:36.1: “What must one attain to be called ‘scholar’?”
translation_text.snp3.6:36.3: “Why is one ‘noble’, and how is one ‘well conducted’?
translation_text.snp3.6:36.5: May the Buddha please answer my question.”
translation_text.snp3.6:37.3: “and has known for themselves what in the world <j>is blameworthy and blameless;
translation_text.snp3.6:38.1: Having cut off defilements and clingings,
translation_text.snp3.6:38.2: being wise, they enter no womb.
translation_text.snp3.6:38.3: They’ve cast aside the bog of the three perceptions,
translation_text.snp3.6:38.4: the one they call ‘noble’ does not return to creation.
translation_text.snp3.6:39.1: One here who is accomplished <j>and skillful in all forms of good conduct;
translation_text.snp3.6:39.3: everywhere not stuck, freed in mind,
translation_text.snp3.6:39.4: who has no repulsion: they are ‘well-conducted’.
translation_text.snp3.6:40.2: having banished any deed that results in suffering,
translation_text.snp3.6:40.3: deceit and conceit, as well as greed and anger,
translation_text.snp3.6:40.5: They have made a limit on name & form;
translation_text.snp3.6:40.6: the one they call a ‘wanderer’ <j>has reached their destination.”
translation_text.snp3.6:41.1: And then, having approved and agreed with what the Buddha said, uplifted and elated, full of rapture and happiness, Sabhiya got up from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his joined palms toward the Buddha, and extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses:
translation_text.snp3.6:42.1: “O one of vast wisdom, <j>there are three & sixty opinions
translation_text.snp3.6:42.2: based on the doctrines of ascetics:
translation_text.snp3.6:42.3: they are expressions of perception, <j>based on perception.
translation_text.snp3.6:42.4: Having dispelled them all, <j>you passed over the dark flood.
translation_text.snp3.6:43.1: You have gone to the end, gone beyond suffering,
translation_text.snp3.6:43.2: you are perfected, a fully awakened Buddha; <j>I think you have ended defilements.
translation_text.snp3.6:43.4: ender of suffering—you brought me across!
translation_text.snp3.6:44.2: you brought me beyond doubt—homage to you!
translation_text.snp3.6:44.3: A sage, accomplished in the ways of sagacity,
translation_text.snp3.6:44.4: you are gentle, not hardhearted, O Kinsman of the Sun.
translation_text.snp3.6:45.1: Any doubts that I once had,
translation_text.snp3.6:45.2: you have answered for me, O Clear-eyed One.
translation_text.snp3.6:45.3: Clearly you are a sage, an Awakened One,
translation_text.snp3.6:45.4: there are no hindrances in you.
translation_text.snp3.6:46.2: is blown away and mown down.
translation_text.snp3.6:47.1: O giant among giants, O great hero,
translation_text.snp3.6:48.2: Homage to you, supreme among men!
translation_text.snp3.6:48.3: In the world with its gods,
translation_text.snp3.6:49.1: You are the Buddha, you are the Teacher,
translation_text.snp3.6:49.2: you are the sage who has overcome Māra;
translation_text.snp3.6:49.3: you have cut off the underlying tendencies,
translation_text.snp3.6:49.4: you’ve crossed over, and you bring humanity across.
translation_text.snp3.6:50.3: you are a lion, free of grasping,
translation_text.snp3.6:50.4: with fear and dread given up.
translation_text.snp3.6:51.2: to which water does not stick,
translation_text.snp3.6:51.5: Stretch out your feet, great hero:
translation_text.snp3.6:52.1: Then the wanderer Sabhiya bowed with his head at the Buddha’s feet and said,
translation_text.snp3.6:52.3: I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.
translation_text.snp3.6:52.4: Sir, may I receive the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence?”
translation_text.snp3.6:53.1: “Sabhiya, if someone formerly ordained in another sect wishes to take the going forth, the ordination in this teaching and training, they must spend four months on probation. When four months have passed, if the mendicants are satisfied, they’ll give the going forth, the ordination into monkhood.
translation_text.snp3.6:53.2: However, I recognize individual differences in this matter.”
translation_text.snp3.6:54.1: “Sir, if four months probation are required in such a case, I’ll spend four years on probation. When four years have passed, if the mendicants are satisfied, let them give me the going forth, the ordination into monkhood.”
translation_text.snp3.6:54.2: And the wanderer Sabhiya received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha’s presence.
translation_text.snp3.6:54.3: And Venerable Sabhiya became one of the perfected.

variant_text.snp3.6:2.2: pakudho → pakuddho (sya-all, km) | sañcayo → sañjayo (bj, sya-all, km, pts-vp-pli1) | belaṭṭhaputto → bellaṭṭhiputto (bj); veḷaṭṭhaputto (sya-all); belaṭṭhiputto (pts-vp-pli1) | nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto → nigaṇṭho nātaputto (bj, pts-vp-pli1)
variant_text.snp3.6:5.2: yepi kho te → ye kho te (sya-all); yaṁ kho te (mr)
variant_text.snp3.6:6.2: samaṇo kho → samaṇo kho gotamo (sya-all, mr)
variant_text.snp3.6:51.2: na upalimpati → toye na upalippati (bj); toye na upalippati (pts-vp-pli1); toyena na upalimpati (mr)
